title: How to Map BC Mineral Claims from the Public Registry
metaDescription: Search British Columbia mineral claims by company or claimholder, import the tenures into Exploration Maps, style them, and export a clean investor-ready map. Includes Mineral Titles Online verification steps.
directAnswer: To map BC mineral claims, open Exploration Maps, search a company or claimholder to pull their British Columbia tenures from public registry data, add the claims to the map, assign the Claims layer role for standard styling, add context and a title block, then export a PNG or PDF. Always confirm current ownership, status, and boundaries in BC Mineral Titles Online (MTO) before relying on the map.

ctaTop.text: Search a claimholder and create a map.
ctaTop.sub: Pull a company's BC tenures and have a styled map in minutes.

ctaBottom.text: Import your file and export an investor-ready map.
ctaBottom.sub: Open the editor, drop in your claims, and export a clean PNG or PDF.

sections.h2: What you'll need
sections.body: You can build a BC claims map two ways: search a claimholder directly inside Exploration Maps, or import a tenure file you already have. Either way it helps to have:
checklist: A company or claimholder name (for the in-app claim search), or
A BC tenure file you exported from MTO or a GIS (GeoJSON, Shapefile, or KML)
Your company logo (PNG or JPG) for the title block
The project name and map date
The official MTO tenure numbers you want to verify

sections.h2: Step 1: Search BC claims by company or claimholder
sections.body: Open Exploration Maps and choose Add Claims → Search Claims Registry. Select British Columbia, then type a company or claimholder name. The tool returns the matching tenures from public BC registry data so you can map a competitor's ground or your own holdings without manually entering coordinates.

image.alt: Claim search panel with British Columbia selected and a company name typed in
image.caption: Searching BC tenures by claimholder name in the Add Claims panel

sections.h2: Step 2: Add the claims to your map
sections.body: Select the tenures (or tenure groups) you want and click Add to map. They're added as a claims layer and the map fits to their extent automatically. If you instead have a tenure file, use the import button in the Layers section and select your GeoJSON, Shapefile, or KML — the layer is added the same way.

cta.text: Turn public claim data into a clean map.
cta.sub: Add a claimholder's BC tenures to the map in one click.

sections.h2: Step 3: Assign the Claims role and style the tenures
sections.body: Expand the layer card and choose 'Claims' from the Role dropdown to apply the standard convention: a medium-blue stroke with a light, semi-transparent blue fill. Adjust the stroke and fill colour pickers if you want to distinguish optioned ground, JV claims, or separate blocks — each layer gets its own legend entry, so rename them to something descriptive like 'Optioned Claims' or 'Zone 1 Tenures'.

image.alt: Layer card showing the Claims role selected with blue stroke and fill
image.caption: Assigning the Claims role applies investor-standard blue styling

sections.h2: Step 4: Add context, a title block, and your logo
sections.body: Enable the Context overlay (roads, towns, water) and Reference Labels in the Design section so viewers can orient the property, and drop the overlay opacity to 40–60% so the claims stay dominant. Click the title on the map to enter the project and company name, set the map date in Design → Text & Metadata, and upload your logo at the bottom of the Layers section.

sections.h2: Step 5: Export a clean BC claims map
sections.body: Pick an export ratio — Landscape 16:9 for slides and news releases, Letter Portrait for reports — frame the claims within the bounds, then export PNG for presentations or PDF for print. Enter your email to unlock watermark-free exports.

image.alt: Export panel with Landscape 16:9 ratio selected over a framed BC claims map
image.caption: Framing and exporting the finished BC claims map

sections.h2: Example: mapping a competitor's Golden Triangle ground
sections.body: Say you want a one-slide map of a peer's claims near the Golden Triangle for a corporate update. Search their company name, select British Columbia, add their tenure group, assign the Claims role, switch to the Satellite basemap to show the terrain, add a title block with your logo and the date, then export Landscape 16:9. Total time is a few minutes versus an afternoon of downloading shapefiles and styling them in a desktop GIS.

sections.h2: Verify everything in Mineral Titles Online
sections.body: Exploration Maps is a visualization and workflow tool, not the official registry. Before you publish or rely on a BC claims map, confirm the details directly in MTO.

box.title: Verify in BC Mineral Titles Online (MTO)
box.html: Always verify current ownership, status, expiry, good-to dates, and boundaries for each tenure number in BC Mineral Titles Online before relying on the map. Tenure status can change daily.

sections.h2: Where the claim data comes from

box.title: Data source & disclaimer
box.html: Pulls from public registry data where available. Some jurisdictions are queried live; others use refreshed public datasets. BC tenure information originates with the Province of British Columbia's Mineral Titles Online. Exploration Maps is not affiliated with or endorsed by the Government of British Columbia and is not the official registry of record.

sections.h2: What the app can and can't verify

box.title: Can and cannot verify
box.html:
Can: show tenure geometry and attributes from public data, let you style and label them, and combine them with your own files.
Cannot: confirm legal ownership, good-standing, work-requirement compliance, or that boundaries are survey-accurate. Treat the map as a visualization, and confirm specifics in MTO.

sections.h2: Related guides
sections.html: Next, see the broader how to make a mining claims map guide, compare workflows in Ontario, learn to import a GeoJSON or Shapefile, and when you're done, export a print-ready PDF. You can also start straight from the BC mineral claims map tool page.

faqs.q: Can I search BC mineral claims by company name?
faqs.a: Yes. In Exploration Maps, choose Add Claims → Search Claims Registry, select British Columbia, and type a company or claimholder name. Matching tenures from public registry data are returned so you can add them to your map without entering coordinates. Always confirm the results against BC Mineral Titles Online.

faqs.q: Is Exploration Maps the official BC claims registry?
faqs.a: No. Exploration Maps is a visualization and workflow tool. BC's official registry of record is Mineral Titles Online (MTO), operated by the Province of British Columbia. Use Exploration Maps to build and export maps, and verify ownership, status, and boundaries in MTO.

faqs.q: How current is the BC claim data?
faqs.a: It reflects public registry data, which may be queried live or drawn from refreshed public datasets depending on the jurisdiction and source. Tenure status can change daily, so always verify current status and good-to dates in MTO before relying on the map.

faqs.q: What file formats can I import for BC tenures?
faqs.a: GeoJSON, Shapefile (.shp, zipped), and KML/KMZ. If you have corner coordinates in a spreadsheet, export it as CSV and use the Column Mapper to assign latitude and longitude. You can convert Shapefiles to GeoJSON for free at mapshaper.org.

faqs.q: Can I show my claims and a competitor's claims on the same map?
faqs.a: Yes. Add each set as its own layer, give them different stroke and fill colours, and rename them so they read clearly in the legend (for example 'Our Claims' and 'Adjacent Tenures'). Each layer appears as a separate legend entry.

faqs.q: What's the standard colour for mineral claims on a BC map?
faqs.a: A medium-blue stroke with a light, semi-transparent blue fill — the convention used in most NI 43-101 technical reports. It's applied automatically when you assign the Claims role, and you can override it per layer.

title: How to Make a Mining Claims Map
metaDescription: The complete guide to making a mining claims map: import public claim data, shapefiles, KML, GeoJSON, or drillhole CSVs, style your claims, and export a clean investor-ready map in minutes. Links to province-by-province search guides.
directAnswer: To make a mining claims map, get your claims into Exploration Maps — either search a claimholder to pull public registry data or import a GeoJSON, Shapefile, or KML — assign the Claims layer role for standard blue styling, add context layers and a title block, then export as PNG or PDF. Exploration Maps is a visualization tool, not the official registry, so verify ownership and boundaries with the relevant provincial registry.

ctaTop.text: Turn public claim data into a clean map.
ctaTop.sub: Search a claimholder or import a file and style it in minutes.

ctaBottom.text: Import your file and export an investor-ready map.
ctaBottom.sub: Open the editor and have a shareable claims map in minutes.

sections.h2: What you need before you start
sections.body: A mining claims map needs claim geometry plus a little context. Gather:
checklist: Claim data: a claimholder name to search, or a boundary file (GeoJSON, Shapefile, or KML)
Optional context: roads, water, or infrastructure files
Your company logo (PNG or JPG)
Project name, map date, and any required disclaimers

sections.h2: Find your province's claim-search guide first
sections.body: The fastest way to map claims is to search a claimholder and pull public registry data straight into the map. Each province's registry works a little differently — start with the guide for your jurisdiction, then come back here to style and export.

sections.h2: Step 1: Get your claims onto the map
sections.body: Open Exploration Maps. To search public data, choose Add Claims → Search Claims Registry, pick your province, and type a company or claimholder name. To use your own file, click the import button in the Layers section and select your GeoJSON, Shapefile, or KML. Either way the layer is added automatically and the map fits to your claims. If your data is corner coordinates in a spreadsheet, import the CSV and the Column Mapper assigns the fields.

image.alt: Layers panel with a claims boundary imported and the map fit to its extent
image.caption: A claims boundary imported and framed automatically

sections.h2: Step 2: Assign the Claims layer role
sections.body: Expand the layer card and select 'Claims' from the Role dropdown. This applies the standard convention automatically: a blue stroke with a light, semi-transparent blue fill — the styling investors and NI 43-101 reviewers expect. Override the stroke and fill colours per layer if you're showing multiple blocks.

image.alt: Layer card with the Claims role selected showing blue claim styling
image.caption: The Claims role applies investor-standard styling in one click

sections.h2: Step 3: Add context layers
sections.body: A claims map without context is hard to read. Enable the Context overlay (roads, towns, water) and Reference Labels in the Design section, and reduce overlay opacity to 40–60% so your claims stay dominant. Add roads or infrastructure files if you have them.

sections.h2: Step 4: Choose a basemap and theme
sections.body: For investor presentations and reports, the Light basemap with the Investor — Navy & White theme is the standard choice. Use the Satellite basemap to show terrain, and the Technical — Sharp Borders theme for formal regulatory filings.

sections.h2: Step 5: Configure the title block and logo
sections.body: Click the title on the map to edit it inline — project name as the title, company name as the subtitle. Add the map date and project number in Design → Text & Metadata, and upload your logo at the bottom of the Layers section.

cta.text: Search a claimholder and create a map.
cta.sub: Pull public tenures for any province and style them instantly.

sections.h2: Step 6: Export your map
sections.body: Activate an export ratio — Landscape 16:9 for slides and news releases, Letter Portrait for technical reports — frame your claims within the bounds, then export PNG for presentations or PDF for print. Enter your email to unlock watermark-free exports.

sections.h2: Example: a two-block property overview
sections.body: Suppose you hold a core claim block and recently optioned a second block. Import (or search) both, put each on its own layer, colour the core block solid blue and the optioned block in a contrasting outline, rename the layers 'Core Claims' and 'Optioned Ground', add roads for access context, drop in your logo and date, and export Landscape 16:9. You now have a clean property overview suitable for a deck or a news release.

sections.h2: Choosing your starting point
Where the data comes from

box.title: Data source & disclaimer
box.html: Pulls from public registry data where available. Some jurisdictions are queried live; others use refreshed public datasets. Exploration Maps is a visualization and workflow tool, not the official registry of record for any province.

sections.h2: Verify before you rely on the map

box.title: Always verify with the official registry
box.html: Always verify current ownership, status, expiry, and boundaries with the official provincial registry before relying on the map — for example BC Mineral Titles Online, Ontario MLAS, Quebec GESTIM, or Newfoundland GeoAtlas.

sections.h2: Related guides
sections.html: Build investor visuals with the investor presentation guide, add drill data with the drill results map guide, learn imports in the GeoJSON / Shapefile guide, and finish with the export to PDF guide.

faqs.q: What file format do I need for mining claims data?
faqs.a: GeoJSON is preferred, and Shapefile (zipped) and KML/KMZ also work. You can convert Shapefiles to GeoJSON for free at mapshaper.org or in QGIS. If you only have corner coordinates in a spreadsheet, export it as CSV and use the Column Mapper to assign latitude and longitude. Or skip files entirely and search a claimholder to pull public registry data.

faqs.q: Do I need to know the claim coordinates, or can I search by company?
faqs.a: You can search by company or claimholder for supported provinces — choose Add Claims → Search Claims Registry, pick the province, and type the name. The matching public tenures are added to your map without entering coordinates. Always verify the results in the official provincial registry.

faqs.q: What is the standard colour for mineral claims on a map?
faqs.a: A medium-blue stroke with a light, semi-transparent blue fill (around 20–25% opacity). It's applied automatically when you assign the Claims role and matches the convention used in most NI 43-101 technical reports.

faqs.q: Can I export a mining claims map as a PDF?
faqs.a: Yes. Choose the PDF export and select your page size (Letter Landscape, A4 Landscape, or News Release Figure). PDFs are vector-quality and suitable for print in regulatory filings and technical reports. See the export to PDF guide for details.

faqs.q: How do I show multiple claim blocks with different colours?
faqs.a: Import or search each block as its own layer, then set a different stroke and fill colour per layer. Each layer becomes a separate legend entry, so rename them to something descriptive like 'Zone 1 Claims' or 'Optioned Claims'.

faqs.q: Is Exploration Maps the official claims registry?
faqs.a: No. It's a visualization and workflow tool that helps you turn public claim data and your own files into clean maps. The official registries of record are the provincial systems (BC MTO, Ontario MLAS, Quebec GESTIM, Newfoundland GeoAtlas, and others). Always verify ownership, status, and boundaries there before relying on the map.

title: How to Make a Drill Results Map
metaDescription: Turn a drill collar CSV into a clean drill results map for news releases and NI 43-101 reports: required columns, fixing coordinate issues, hole-ID callouts and intercept labels, and a print-ready export.
directAnswer: To make a drill results map, import your drill collar CSV, map the latitude/longitude (or easting/northing) columns, assign the Drillholes role, add callout labels showing hole ID and best intercept, add the property boundary, then export as a Landscape 16:9 PNG or a news-release figure PDF.

ctaTop.text: Import your file and export an investor-ready map.
ctaTop.sub: Drop in a collar CSV and label your best holes in minutes.

ctaBottom.text: Turn drill data into a clean map.
ctaBottom.sub: Open the editor, import your collars, and export a news-release figure.

sections.h2: What data you need
sections.body: A drill results map is built from a collar table plus your headline intercepts. Gather:
checklist: A drill collar CSV (one row per hole) with hole ID and coordinates
Your best intercept per key hole (width × grade) for the labels
A property/claims boundary file for context (GeoJSON, Shapefile, or KML)
Your company logo and the program/map date

sections.h2: Required and recommended CSV columns
sections.body: At minimum you need a hole ID and a coordinate pair. Everything else is preserved as feature properties you can show in callouts.

sections.h2: Step 1: Import the drill collar CSV
sections.body: Click the import button in the Layers section and select your collar CSV. The Column Mapper opens automatically — assign latitude and longitude. Columns named Lat, Long, Latitude, Longitude, Easting, or Northing are detected for you. Every other column (hole ID, depth, grade) is preserved and accessible when you click a hole.

image.alt: Column Mapper showing latitude and longitude columns being assigned from a collar CSV
image.caption: Mapping coordinate columns when importing a collar CSV

sections.h2: Common coordinate issues (and fixes)
sections.body: Most drill maps that come out wrong are a coordinate problem, not a styling problem.
checklist: Lat/long swapped: if holes land in the wrong hemisphere, you've mapped longitude to latitude — re-map the columns
UTM vs decimal degrees: easting/northing in the hundreds of thousands won't plot — convert to decimal degrees (WGS84) first, then import
Sign dropped: western/southern coordinates must be negative (e.g. -122.5, not 122.5)
Datum mismatch: NAD83 vs WGS84 is usually sub-metre, but a wrong datum or zone can shift holes by hundreds of metres — verify against a known landmark

sections.h2: Step 2: Assign the Drillholes role
sections.body: Expand the layer card and select 'Drillholes' from the Role dropdown for standard dark circle markers. A marker size of 12–16 px reads well at most scales.

image.alt: Drillholes role selected on a layer of collar points over a property
image.caption: Collars styled with the Drillholes role

sections.h2: Step 3: Add hole-ID and intercept callouts
sections.body: Click a hole to open the inline feature editor. Enter the hole ID as the title and the best intercept as the subtext — for example '15.2m @ 4.8 g/t Au' — then Add / Update Callout. For your top results, switch the callout type to 'Badge Label' and put the grade in the badge chip so the standout holes pop. Use the Priority field to keep labels from colliding on dense programs.

sections.h2: Step 4: Add the property boundary
sections.body: Import your claims boundary as a second layer and assign the Claims role. This gives the drill pattern the property context investors and analysts expect to see.

cta.text: Turn public claim data into a clean map.
cta.sub: Add your claims boundary around the drill pattern in seconds.

sections.h2: Step 5: Frame and export for a news release
sections.body: Activate Landscape 16:9 — the standard for news-release figures — and frame so all holes and key callouts fit. The Investor — Navy & White theme with a Light or Satellite basemap is the convention. Export PNG at 2× for email and web, or the News Release Figure PDF (6" × 4.5") for print-ready delivery.

sections.h2: Example: a 12-hole discovery program
sections.body: You've finished a 12-hole program with three standout intercepts. Import the collar CSV, map the coordinates, assign Drillholes, badge-label the three best holes with their grades, add plain callouts on the rest, drop in the claims boundary and your logo, switch to Satellite to show the ridge the holes follow, and export Landscape 16:9. That figure is ready to attach to the news release and reuse in the deck.

sections.h2: About the data and what to disclose

box.title: Can and cannot verify
box.html:
Can: plot the collars you provide, preserve your attributes, and label intercepts exactly as you enter them.
Cannot: validate assays, QA/QC, or that an intercept is true width. The map reflects the data you import — make sure your disclosure follows your QP's guidance and applicable securities rules.

sections.h2: Related guides
sections.html: See the news-release figure workflow, the broad mining claims map guide, how to import CSV data, and how to export a print-ready PDF.

faqs.q: What columns does my drill collar CSV need?
faqs.a: At minimum a hole ID and a latitude/longitude pair in decimal degrees (WGS84). If your collars are in UTM, convert them to decimal degrees first. Total depth and best intercept are recommended because they feed your callouts, and any extra columns are kept as feature properties you can display.

faqs.q: My holes plotted in the wrong place — what happened?
faqs.a: Almost always a coordinate issue: latitude and longitude swapped, a missing negative sign on western/southern coordinates, or UTM easting/northing imported as if they were decimal degrees. Convert UTM to decimal degrees (WGS84) first, re-open the Column Mapper, confirm the columns, and re-import.

faqs.q: What format should drill results be shown in on a map?
faqs.a: The standard is '[width]m @ [grade] [unit] [element]' — for example '15.2m @ 4.8 g/t Au' or '82m @ 0.92% Cu'. Put the hole ID as the callout title and the intercept as the subtext.

faqs.q: How many drill holes should I label?
faqs.a: Label all current-program holes plus key historical holes for context. If it gets crowded past ~20 holes, use the Priority field to control which callouts render on top and reserve Badge Labels for your top 3–5 intercepts.

faqs.q: Should I use a satellite or light basemap?
faqs.a: Both work. Satellite conveys terrain and setting and is popular for news releases; Light is cleaner for formal technical reports and PDF attachments in NI 43-101 documents.

faqs.q: Can I show historical and current holes differently?
faqs.a: Yes — import them as separate layers and style them differently (filled dark markers for current, hollow or lighter for historical). Name the layers so they appear as separate legend entries.

title: How to Create an Exploration Map for an Investor Presentation
metaDescription: Build investor-ready exploration maps for decks, project overviews, and news releases: pick a branded theme, set a 16:9 layout, keep the legend clean, add callouts, and export sharp PNGs and PDFs.
directAnswer: For an investor presentation map, use a branded theme (such as Investor — Navy & White), the Landscape 16:9 ratio, a satellite or light basemap, a concise legend, and callouts for your key results — then export PNG at 2× resolution. Most decks use three maps: a regional location map, a property/claims overview, and a results map.

ctaTop.text: Import your file and export an investor-ready map.
ctaTop.sub: Build a deck-ready map with your brand colours and logo in minutes.

ctaBottom.text: Turn public claim data into a clean map.
ctaBottom.sub: Open the editor and export sharp PNGs and PDFs for your deck.

sections.h2: Why map quality matters in mining investor decks
sections.body: Professional investors and analysts evaluate dozens of exploration companies. A clean, consistent, on-brand map signals operational competence; a blurry or cluttered one undermines confidence in the technical team. Your map should communicate the property's merits at a glance — even on a projector from the back of the room.

sections.h2: The three maps most decks need
sections.body: Rather than reusing one map everywhere, build a small set that each does one job.
checklist: Regional location map — where the project sits relative to known camps and infrastructure
Property / claims overview — your tenure footprint with a clean legend
Results or targets map — drill intercepts or target areas with callouts

sections.h2: Step 1: Brand the map with a theme, colours, and logo
sections.body: Pick a theme such as Investor — Navy & White, then apply your own brand colours and logo so every map in the deck matches. Saving a brand kit means the next project starts on-brand automatically. Click the title to set the project and company name, and add the date in Design → Text & Metadata.

image.alt: Map with a navy title block, company logo, and branded colours applied
image.caption: A branded title block keeps every deck map consistent

sections.h2: Step 2: Set the Landscape 16:9 ratio
sections.body: Activate Landscape 16:9 in the Export section before framing — it matches the standard PowerPoint and Keynote slide, so the map fills the slide with no letterboxing. The frame shows exactly what will export, so pan and zoom to taste.

image.alt: Export panel with Landscape 16:9 selected and the map framed to the slide
image.caption: Framing to the 16:9 slide ratio before export

sections.h2: Step 3: Keep the legend scannable
sections.body: Decks are read at speed. Disable legend entries the audience doesn't need, use the Compact legend mode, and if you have more than ~5 entries, split into two slides (overview + detail) rather than crowding one map.

cta.text: Turn public claim data into a clean map.
cta.sub: Pull your tenures and build a clean property-overview slide.

sections.h2: Step 4: Add callouts for your headline results
sections.body: Use Badge Label callouts for top drill intercepts so they pop, and plain labels for target names. Keep text short — '32m @ 6.1 g/t Au' beats a full sentence. These are the numbers the room remembers.

sections.h2: Step 5: Export sharp assets for the deck
sections.body: Export PNG at 2× pixel ratio (a ~3840×2160 image — sharp on screen and in print handouts) and insert it as an image so it travels with the deck. For investor one-pagers and printed overviews, export a PDF instead. Reuse the same maps in news releases for a consistent look.

sections.h2: Example: a financing-roadshow deck
sections.body: Heading into a financing, you need three slides. Make a regional location map on Satellite showing proximity to a producing mine, a claims overview on Light with a compact legend, and a results map badge-labelling your three best holes. Brand all three with one kit, export each at 16:9 2×, and you have a consistent map set for the deck, the one-pager, and the website — built in well under an hour.

sections.h2: A note on commercial use and exports

box.title: Exports & commercial use
box.html: Exploration Maps is built for commercial exploration work — investor decks, project overviews, and news-release figures. Watermark-free exports unlock with your email, and paid export/subscription options may be offered. See the export guide for formats and resolutions.

sections.h2: Verify the underlying data

box.title: Verify before you present
box.html: If your map shows claims or tenures, always verify current ownership, status, and boundaries with the official registry before presenting to investors. Exploration Maps is a visualization tool, not the official registry.

sections.h2: Related guides
sections.html: Build the underlying maps with the mining claims map guide and the drill results map guide, prep a release figure with the news-release guide, and export with the PDF export guide.

faqs.q: What size should a mining map be in a PowerPoint slide?
faqs.a: Export at Landscape 16:9 with 2× pixel ratio. That yields roughly a 3840×2160 image — sharp at any zoom. Insert it as a full-slide image with no white border.

faqs.q: How many maps should I include in an investor presentation?
faqs.a: Usually 2–3: a regional location map, a property/claims overview, and a detailed drill-results or target map. Each does a different job — avoid reusing one map for all three.

faqs.q: How do I keep all my deck maps looking consistent?
faqs.a: Apply the same theme, brand colours, and logo to each map. Saving a brand kit lets new projects start on-brand automatically, so a regional map, a claims map, and a results map all match.

faqs.q: Should the company logo be on the map itself?
faqs.a: Yes for anything that leaves a controlled deck — news releases, website downloads, email attachments. On a branded slide template the deck logo may be enough, but a logo on the map protects the asset when it's extracted and reused.

faqs.q: Can I use these maps in a news release as well as a deck?
faqs.a: Yes — that's a common workflow. Export the same map at 16:9 PNG for the deck and as a news-release figure PDF for the release, so the investor materials stay visually consistent. See the news-release guide for the figure workflow.

title: How to Import GeoJSON Data into a Mining Map
metaDescription: Complete guide to importing GeoJSON files into Exploration Maps for mining claims, drill holes, targets, and geological features.
directAnswer: To import GeoJSON into Exploration Maps, click the import button in the Layers section, select your .geojson or .json file, and the layer is added automatically with the map fitting to your data bounds.

sections.h2: What is GeoJSON and Why It's Used for Mining Maps
sections.body: GeoJSON is an open standard format for encoding geographic data — polygons, lines, and points — as JSON. It is the most widely compatible format for web-based mapping tools. Most GIS software (QGIS, ArcGIS, MapInfo) can export to GeoJSON, and government mineral title databases often provide boundary data in GeoJSON or Shapefile format (which can be converted).

sections.h2: Converting Shapefiles to GeoJSON
sections.body: If your data is in Shapefile format (.shp, .dbf, .shx, .prj), convert it to GeoJSON before importing. The free online tool at mapshaper.org accepts Shapefile ZIP archives and exports clean GeoJSON in seconds. QGIS users can right-click any layer and choose Export → Save Features As → GeoJSON.

sections.h2: Importing Your GeoJSON File
sections.body: In Exploration Maps, click the upload/import icon in the Layers section of the sidebar. Select your .geojson or .json file. The layer is added immediately and the map automatically fits to the extent of your data. If your file contains multiple geometry types (polygons and lines, for example), they are split into separate layers automatically.

sections.h2: Assigning a Layer Role After Import
sections.body: After importing, expand the layer card and assign a Role. Roles drive automatic styling — Claims applies blue boundaries, Drillholes applies dark circle markers, Target Areas applies amber dashed outlines. Assigning the correct role saves significant manual styling time and ensures your map follows industry conventions.

sections.h2: Checking Coordinate Systems
sections.body: Exploration Maps expects geographic coordinates in WGS84 (EPSG:4326 — standard latitude/longitude). If your GeoJSON displays in the wrong location or far off from expected, the source data may be in a projected coordinate system (UTM, NAD83). Re-export from your GIS software with the coordinate system set to WGS84 / EPSG:4326.

faqs.q: What file extensions does Exploration Maps accept?
faqs.a: For vector data: .geojson and .json. For tabular point data: .csv. For images (logo and inset maps): .jpg and .png. Shapefiles must be converted to GeoJSON first.

faqs.q: My GeoJSON file shows in the wrong location — what's wrong?
faqs.a: The most common cause is that the data is in a projected coordinate system (such as UTM Zone 10N or BC Albers) rather than geographic coordinates (WGS84 latitude/longitude). Re-export from your GIS software, specifying WGS84 / EPSG:4326 as the output coordinate reference system.

faqs.q: Can I import multiple GeoJSON files?
faqs.a: Yes — import as many files as needed. Each file becomes a separate layer in the Layers list. You can toggle visibility, reorder layers (drag to change draw order), and style each layer independently.

title: How to Choose the Right Basemap for a Mining Map
metaDescription: Guide to choosing between light, satellite, topographic, and dark basemaps for mining exploration maps in investor presentations, technical reports, and news releases.
directAnswer: Use the Light basemap for NI 43-101 reports and regulatory filings, Satellite for investor presentations and location maps showing terrain, Topographic for infrastructure and access maps, and Dark for digital dashboards and social media.

sections.h2: The Four Basemaps and When to Use Each
sections.body: Exploration Maps offers four basemap styles. Each has a specific context where it performs best:

sections.h2: Light Basemap: Technical Reports and Regulatory Filings
sections.body: The Light basemap uses a neutral light gray background that makes coloured data layers — claims boundaries, drill holes, target areas — stand out clearly. It is the standard choice for NI 43-101 technical reports, regulatory submissions, and any map where the data must be legible in black-and-white printing. Pair with the Technical — Sharp Borders theme for formal documents.

sections.h2: Satellite Basemap: Investor Presentations and Location Maps
sections.body: The Satellite basemap shows real aerial imagery, giving investors immediate visual context for terrain, vegetation, access roads, and infrastructure. It is the most compelling basemap for investor materials and is especially effective for properties with distinct topographic features. Reduce the Reference Labels overlay opacity to 50% so place names are readable without overwhelming the imagery.

sections.h2: Topographic Basemap: Infrastructure and Field Maps
sections.body: The Topographic basemap shows contour lines and elevation shading. It is ideal for infrastructure maps (road access, drill site planning), environmental assessments, and field geology maps where elevation context matters. Pair with the Terrain — Earthy & Warm theme for a cohesive look.

sections.h2: Dark Basemap: Digital Presentations and Social Media
sections.body: The Dark basemap provides a high-contrast background that makes coloured data layers glow. It is best for digital-first content: website graphics, social media posts, and on-screen investor day presentations. It is not suitable for printed materials or formal technical documents.

sections.h2: Reference Overlays: Adding Context to Any Basemap
sections.body: Regardless of which basemap you choose, the Context overlay adds roads, water bodies, and towns. The Reference Labels overlay adds place names. The Railway Network overlay adds rail infrastructure. These overlays work on top of any basemap and can be fine-tuned with the Overlay Opacity slider.

faqs.q: Which basemap is required for NI 43-101 reports?
faqs.a: NI 43-101 does not specify a particular basemap style, but convention strongly favours the Light or Topographic basemap for technical maps. The requirement is that the map be legible, have a north arrow, scale bar, and appropriate legend — the style is at the discretion of the Qualified Person.

faqs.q: Can I use satellite imagery in a printed report?
faqs.a: Yes. Satellite basemaps export at full resolution in PNG and PDF formats, suitable for print. Ensure your export pixel ratio is set to 2× or 3× for print-quality output.

faqs.q: What basemap works best for a regional location map?
faqs.a: The Satellite basemap is most commonly used for regional location maps — it helps investors immediately understand the property's physical setting and surrounding terrain. The Topographic basemap is also effective for mountainous or complex terrain.

title: How to Export a Mining Map as a PDF
metaDescription: Export high-quality PDF mining maps for NI 43-101 reports, news releases, and regulatory filings. Guide covering page sizes, pixel ratio, and print settings.
directAnswer: To export a mining map as PDF in Exploration Maps, click the PDF button in the toolbar, choose your page size (Letter, A4, or News Release Figure), enter your email to remove the watermark, and download.

sections.h2: PDF vs PNG: Which to Use
sections.body: Use PDF for documents that will be printed or submitted to regulators — NI 43-101 reports, permitting applications, environmental assessments. Use PNG for digital use — PowerPoint slides, email attachments, website images, and news release wire service submissions. Both formats export at full resolution with all panels (title, legend, logo) included.

sections.h2: Choosing the Right PDF Page Size
sections.body: Exploration Maps offers five PDF page sizes: PowerPoint 16:9 (13.33" × 7.5") for presentation-ready PDFs, Letter Landscape (11" × 8.5") and Portrait (8.5" × 11") for North American regulatory filings, A4 Landscape and Portrait for international filings and European investors, and News Release Figure (6" × 4.5") for figures attached to press releases. Choose Letter or A4 Portrait for NI 43-101 appendix maps.

sections.h2: Setting the Export Ratio Before Exporting PDF
sections.body: Activate the appropriate ratio in the Export section before clicking PDF. For Letter Portrait PDFs, use the Portrait 3:4 ratio. For Landscape PDFs, use Landscape 16:9. This ensures your map fills the page without letterboxing. Pan and zoom to frame your map within the ratio bounds — the dashed border shows the export frame.

sections.h2: Watermark-Free Export
sections.body: Enter your work email in the export dialog to unlock clean PDF exports with no explorationmaps.com watermark. Your email is stored locally and remembered for future sessions. A 'Download with watermark' option is always available without entering an email if you need a quick draft.

sections.h2: Pixel Ratio for Print-Quality PDFs
sections.body: Set the Pixel Ratio to 3× in Export Settings for any map that will be printed. 3× produces an image embedded in the PDF at approximately 300 DPI at standard page sizes — the minimum for professional print quality. 2× is sufficient for screen-only use.

faqs.q: What PDF page size should I use for a NI 43-101 technical report?
faqs.a: NI 43-101 technical reports are typically formatted on Letter (8.5" × 11") or A4 (8.27" × 11.69") paper. Use the Letter Portrait or A4 Portrait PDF page size. For larger foldout maps included as appendices, use Letter Landscape.

faqs.q: Can I embed a PDF mining map into a Word document?
faqs.a: Yes. In Word, use Insert → Object → Create from File to embed the PDF, or insert the PNG export as an image (which is simpler for most users). For NI 43-101 reports where the map is a separate appendix, the PDF file itself is typically submitted as a separate attachment.

faqs.q: What resolution is the exported PDF?
faqs.a: PDFs from Exploration Maps contain a raster image at the resolution set by your Pixel Ratio setting. At 2× ratio and Letter Portrait size, the embedded image is approximately 200 DPI — suitable for most professional printing. At 3×, it approaches 300 DPI — the standard for high-quality print.

title: How to Add a Distance Ring to a Mining Map
metaDescription: Add distance rings to exploration maps to show radius from a key point — drill target, town, or infrastructure. Step-by-step guide with arc labels.
directAnswer: To add a distance ring, click 'Draw Distance Ring' in the Annotations section, click the center point on the map, then set the radius in kilometres and add an arc label to show the distance.

sections.h2: When to Use Distance Rings on Mining Maps
sections.body: Distance rings communicate scale and proximity — how far the project is from a town, port, mill, or power line. They are commonly used on location maps to show the project is within a certain radius of infrastructure, and on drill plan maps to show target spacing.

sections.h2: Placing the Ring
sections.body: Click 'Draw Distance Ring' in the Annotations section of the sidebar. Click anywhere on the map to place the ring center. A ring appears immediately — adjust the radius in kilometres using the sidebar input.

sections.h2: Adding an Arc Label
sections.body: Enable 'Curved arc label' in the ring controls. Enter the label text (e.g., '50 km from Port of Prince Rupert'). The label curves along the ring perimeter. Drag the label text or use the Angle slider to position it at any point around the ring — 0° is the top.

sections.h2: Using Outside Shade for Focus
sections.body: Enable 'Outside shade' to add a translucent dark or light vignette over everything outside the ring. This focuses the viewer's attention on the interior area. Use the Dark preset (35% opacity) for a strong focus effect, or Light (30%) for a softer fog effect over satellite imagery.

faqs.q: How do I show a specific distance from a town or port?
faqs.a: Place the ring center on the town or port location, set the radius to the desired distance in km, and add an arc label showing the distance and reference point. For example: '45 km from Stewart, BC'.

faqs.q: Can I add multiple rings at different radii?
faqs.a: Yes — click 'Draw Distance Ring' multiple times to place multiple rings. Each ring is independently configurable with its own radius, color, and label.

title: How to Add a Scale Bar and North Arrow to a Mining Map
metaDescription: Add a scale bar and north arrow to your exploration map — required for NI 43-101 technical reports and regulatory submissions. Quick guide.
directAnswer: Toggle the Scale Bar and North Arrow on in the Design section of the sidebar. They appear on the map automatically and can be repositioned using the corner picker controls.

sections.h2: Why Scale Bars and North Arrows Are Required
sections.body: NI 43-101 technical reports and most regulatory submissions require maps to include a scale bar and north arrow. These elements allow reviewers to measure distances and understand map orientation without additional tools. Without them, maps in technical documents are considered incomplete.

sections.h2: Enabling the Scale Bar
sections.body: Open the Design section in the sidebar and toggle the Scale Bar switch on. The scale bar appears on the map and calculates the displayed distance automatically based on the current zoom level. As you zoom in or out, the scale bar updates in real time. The scale bar is included in all exports (PNG, SVG, PDF).

sections.h2: Enabling the North Arrow
sections.body: Toggle the North Arrow switch on in the Design section. The north arrow appears in the corner you specify using the corner picker. Position it in a corner that doesn't conflict with other panels (title, legend, logo, inset).

sections.h2: Positioning Both Elements
sections.body: Use the corner pickers in the Design section to assign the scale bar and north arrow to specific corners. Elements assigned to the same corner stack automatically without overlapping. Adjust Safe Margins to control how close elements can get to the map edge.

faqs.q: Is a scale bar required for NI 43-101 maps?
faqs.a: Yes. NI 43-101 Form 43-101F1 specifies that all maps included in technical reports must have a scale indicator (scale bar or representative fraction), north arrow, and legend. Maps without these elements may result in comments from the reviewing securities commission.

faqs.q: Does the scale bar update when I zoom the map?
faqs.a: Yes. The scale bar calculates the distance it represents based on the current zoom level and map projection. If you zoom in, the scale bar shows a shorter distance; zoom out and it shows a longer distance. This means you should set your final zoom level before exporting.

title: How to Create a Target Generation Map
metaDescription: Step-by-step guide to creating a target generation map for exploration programs. Show anomalies, structural features, and priority targets for investor and technical presentations.
directAnswer: To create a target generation map, import your target polygon outlines as GeoJSON, assign the Target Areas role, add anomaly and structural layers, apply outside shading to focus attention on priority zones, label each target, and export at high resolution for technical presentations.

sections.h2: What You Need Before You Start
sections.body: Gather the following before building your target generation map:
items: Target polygon outlines as GeoJSON (digitized from geophysics interpretation or structural mapping)
Anomaly extents as a separate GeoJSON layer (if available)
Fault and structural linework as GeoJSON

sections.h2: Step 1: Import Target Polygon Data
sections.body: Open Exploration Maps and click the import button. Select your target outlines GeoJSON — each polygon should represent a discrete exploration target. The layer is added and the map fits to your data extent automatically. If you have multiple target tiers (e.g., Priority 1, Priority 2), import each as a separate layer so you can style them independently.

sections.h2: Step 2: Assign the Target Areas Role
sections.body: Expand the layer card and select 'Target Areas' from the Role dropdown. This applies a warm orange stroke with a light fill — the conventional styling for exploration targets. For your highest-priority targets, switch the fill to a more saturated colour and increase the stroke weight to 2–3 px. For lower-priority targets, reduce opacity to 40–60% so the hierarchy is visually clear at a glance.

sections.h2: Step 3: Add Anomaly and Structural Layers
sections.body: Import your geophysical anomaly extents and fault or structural linework as separate GeoJSON layers. Assign anomaly polygons a high-contrast fill (magenta or gold works well at low opacity) to communicate the extent of the geophysical signal. Assign fault lines a dashed dark stroke. Reducing the opacity on both to 50–70% keeps these layers readable without overwhelming the target polygons in the foreground.

sections.h2: Step 4: Apply Outside Shade to Focus Attention
sections.body: In the Design section, enable the Outside Shade effect on your priority target layers. This darkens the map area outside the target polygon, drawing the viewer's eye directly to the area of interest. Use it selectively — applying it to too many layers simultaneously reduces the effect. Outside Shade is particularly effective when presenting a single high-priority target to investors or JV partners.

sections.h2: Step 5: Label Each Target
sections.body: Click on each target polygon to open the inline feature editor. Enter the target name (e.g., 'Zone A', 'North Anomaly', 'Western Shear') as the label. For targets with associated geochemical peak values or historical resource estimates, add a brief subtext note. Labels anchor to the polygon centroid automatically — drag them to adjust positioning if polygons overlap.

sections.h2: Step 6: Export for Technical Presentations
sections.body: Target generation maps are typically shown in technical investor presentations and JV partner packages, so the 16:9 Landscape ratio is standard. Select the Satellite basemap to provide terrain and vegetation context. Apply the Technical — Sharp Borders or Modern — Dark Indigo theme. Export at 3× resolution (PNG) for high-resolution display on large screens and in printed technical documents.

faqs.q: What data format do I need for target polygon outlines?
faqs.a: GeoJSON is the required format. If your target outlines are in a GIS package (QGIS, ArcGIS, or MapInfo), export the layer as GeoJSON with CRS set to EPSG:4326 (WGS84). If you've sketched targets in a PDF interpretation, you'll need to digitize them in QGIS first — draw your polygon outlines, then export as GeoJSON.

faqs.q: How do I show multiple priority levels (Priority 1, Priority 2, Priority 3) on the same map?
faqs.a: Import each priority tier as a separate GeoJSON layer. Style Priority 1 targets with a fully saturated colour and 100% opacity. Apply 60% opacity to Priority 2 and 30–40% opacity to Priority 3. This creates a clear visual hierarchy that communicates target ranking without requiring a written legend explanation.

faqs.q: Can I show geophysical anomaly extents (magnetic, IP) alongside the target polygons?
faqs.a: Yes. Import your anomaly extents as a separate GeoJSON polygon layer. Use a semi-transparent fill (20–30% opacity) in a contrasting colour — gold or magenta works well against a satellite basemap. Place the anomaly layer below the target layer in the layer stack so the target outlines render on top.

faqs.q: What export resolution is needed for investor technical presentations?
faqs.a: Export at 3× (PNG) for technical presentations intended for display at full screen on a projector or large monitor. This produces a PNG at approximately 5760 × 3240 pixels for a 16:9 export, which is sufficient for any current display and prints cleanly to A3 or tabloid size.

faqs.q: Should I use a satellite or topographic basemap for a target generation map?
faqs.a: Satellite is the preferred basemap for target generation maps — it communicates terrain, vegetation cover, and outcrop distribution in a single layer, all of which are relevant to the geological story. Use Topographic if your property is in a heavily forested area where satellite imagery doesn't show ground features clearly, or if the structural linework is the primary focus and needs a clean background.

title: How to Create an Infrastructure Map for a Mining Property
metaDescription: Step-by-step guide to creating a mining property infrastructure map showing road access, power, water, and logistics. Used in permitting applications, feasibility studies, and investor presentations.
directAnswer: To create an infrastructure map, import road network and water body GeoJSON, assign Roads and Rivers roles, enable the topographic basemap, add labels for key infrastructure points (airstrip, camp, power line, nearest town), include a scale bar, and export as PDF for permitting packages.

sections.h2: What You Need Before You Start
sections.body: Prepare the following before building your infrastructure map:
items: Road network GeoJSON (from OpenStreetMap export or your GIS package)
Coordinates of key infrastructure points: airstrip, camp, power line corridor, nearest service community

sections.h2: Step 1: Import Road and Access Data
sections.body: Click the import button and select your road network GeoJSON. Assign the Roads role — this applies a medium-weight grey stroke appropriate for showing access routes. If you have multiple road types (paved highway vs. gravel access road vs. exploration track), import each as a separate layer and differentiate by stroke weight: 3 px for highways, 2 px for gravel roads, 1 px dashed for exploration tracks.

sections.h2: Step 2: Add Water Bodies and Rivers
sections.body: Import your water body and river GeoJSON and assign the Rivers role. Water features are a critical component of infrastructure maps for permitting — they show watercourse crossings, proximity to water licences, and drainage basin context. Enable the Context overlay in the Design section as a supplemental water layer if your imported data has gaps.

sections.h2: Step 3: Import the Property Boundary
sections.body: Import your claims boundary as a GeoJSON and assign the Claims role. Reduce the fill opacity to 15–20% so the property boundary is visible without obscuring the infrastructure layers beneath it. The Claims boundary establishes the relationship between your property extent and the surrounding infrastructure — the core message of this map type.

sections.h2: Step 4: Configure the Topographic Basemap
sections.body: In the Design section, select the Topographic basemap. For infrastructure maps, topography is more informative than satellite imagery — contour lines communicate the terrain challenges (ridge crossings, valley floors, slope angles) that determine infrastructure routing and access costs. Apply the Terrain — Earthy & Warm or Technical — Sharp Borders theme, both of which are calibrated for topographic basemaps.

sections.h2: Step 5: Add Infrastructure Labels
sections.body: Click on the map at each key infrastructure point to add a label. Common labels for infrastructure maps: nearest town and distance (e.g., 'Stewart, BC — 45 km'), port or railhead, airstrip or helicopter pad, power line, diesel fuel depot. For linear infrastructure (roads, power lines), use the route label type which places the label along the line rather than at a point. Keep label text concise — infrastructure maps are read quickly and need to communicate logistics at a glance.

sections.h2: Step 6: Export for Permitting Packages
sections.body: Infrastructure maps submitted with permitting applications (environmental assessments, mine permits, reclamation plans) should be exported as PDF in Letter Landscape or A3 format. Enable the scale bar — distance accuracy is particularly important on infrastructure maps where reviewers need to verify road distances and setbacks from watercourses. Enable the North Arrow. Export at print-ready quality for formal submissions.

faqs.q: What file format do I need for road network data?
faqs.a: GeoJSON is required. You can export road data from OpenStreetMap using the Overpass Turbo tool (overpass-turbo.eu) — filter for highway types and export as GeoJSON. If you have road data in a shapefile from a government dataset, convert it to GeoJSON using QGIS (right-click layer → Export → Save Features As → GeoJSON, CRS: EPSG:4326).

faqs.q: Does the map need a scale bar for permitting submissions?
faqs.a: Yes — a scale bar is a standard requirement for maps submitted with permitting applications, environmental assessments, and reclamation plan documents. Toggle it on in the Design section. The scale bar in Exploration Maps updates automatically as you zoom, so it always reflects the current display scale accurately.

faqs.q: What basemap is best for infrastructure maps?
faqs.a: Topographic is the best basemap for infrastructure maps — it shows terrain context and contour lines that are directly relevant to road and infrastructure routing. Satellite is a good alternative in areas with sparse vegetation where ground features are visible from imagery. For formal permitting documents, Topographic is the more accepted convention.

faqs.q: How do I show distance to the nearest highway or service community?
faqs.a: Add a distance ring centred on your property boundary or camp location — this visually communicates the radius to nearby infrastructure. Alternatively, add a point label at the nearest town or highway junction with the approximate road distance in kilometres in the label subtext. Both approaches are commonly used in investor and permitting documentation.

faqs.q: Can I show a power line corridor and camp location on the same map?
faqs.a: Yes. Import your power line corridor as a line GeoJSON layer and style it with a dashed orange stroke — the conventional mapping symbol for power lines. Add a point label at the camp location. If you don't have a GeoJSON for the power line, you can digitize it by exporting a manually drawn line from QGIS or Google Earth Pro as GeoJSON and importing it into Exploration Maps.

title: How to Import CSV Data into a Mining Map
metaDescription: Import drill collars, soil geochem, and rock samples from a CSV into Exploration Maps: required columns, fixing UTM and lat/long coordinate issues, labelling points, and exporting a clean map.
directAnswer: To import CSV data into a mining map, click import and select your CSV — the Column Mapper opens automatically. Assign your latitude and longitude columns (in decimal degrees), click Import, and your points render immediately. Every other column is preserved as a feature property you can use for labels and callouts.

ctaTop.text: Import your file and export an investor-ready map.
ctaTop.sub: Drop in a CSV, map the coordinates, and style it in minutes.

ctaBottom.text: Turn your spreadsheet into a clean map.
ctaBottom.sub: Open the editor, import your CSV, and export a PNG or PDF.

sections.h2: What CSV data works with Exploration Maps
sections.body: Any CSV with a coordinate pair works. The most common exploration uses are:
checklist: Drill collars: Hole ID, Latitude, Longitude, Total Depth, Best Intercept
Soil geochemistry: Sample ID, Latitude, Longitude, Au (ppb), As (ppm)
Rock samples: Sample ID, Latitude, Longitude, Rock Type, Grade
Prospects / showings: Name, Latitude, Longitude, Type, Notes
Historical workings: Name, Latitude, Longitude, Type (shaft, adit, open cut)

sections.h2: Required and recommended columns
sections.body: Only the coordinate pair is strictly required. Column names can be anything — you assign them in the Column Mapper.

rows: Decimal degrees; negative in the west
Grade, depth, element — kept as properties
Useful for styling or splitting layers

sections.h2: Step 1: Prepare your CSV
sections.body: Use decimal degrees (e.g. 49.2827, -123.1207). The first row must be headers, with no blank rows above it, and any text fields containing commas should be wrapped in double quotes. If your data is in UTM (easting/northing), convert it to decimal degrees first — see the coordinate-issues section below.

sections.h2: Step 2: Import and open the Column Mapper
sections.body: Click the import button in the Layers section and select your CSV. The Column Mapper opens automatically, listing every detected column with dropdowns to assign roles.

image.alt: Column Mapper listing CSV columns with latitude and longitude dropdowns
image.caption: The Column Mapper opens automatically for any imported CSV

sections.h2: Step 3: Assign latitude and longitude
sections.body: Use the Latitude and Longitude dropdowns. Columns named Lat, Latitude, Y, or POINT_Y (and Long, Longitude, X, POINT_X) are auto-detected; assign anything else manually. Click Import and your points render on the map immediately.

cta.text: Turn your spreadsheet into a clean map.
cta.sub: Map your coordinate columns and see points appear instantly.

sections.h2: Step 4: Set the role and style
sections.body: Expand the layer and assign a role: Drillholes for collars, Anomalies for geochem, or Default for general points. Override marker colour, size, and shape in the styling controls. For soil results, sizing markers by grade communicates spatial distribution at a glance.

image.alt: Imported point layer with a role assigned and marker size set
image.caption: Assigning a role and styling imported points

sections.h2: Step 5: Label points and add callouts
sections.body: Click any point to open the inline feature editor — all your CSV columns are available as properties. Put the hole or sample ID in the label, and for drill holes put the best intercept in the subtext (e.g. '15.2m @ 4.8 g/t Au'). For headline results, use a Badge Label so the number stands out.

sections.h2: Common coordinate issues and fixes
sections.body: Most failed imports are coordinate problems, not file problems.
checklist: UTM instead of decimal degrees: easting/northing in the hundreds of thousands won't plot — convert to decimal degrees (WGS84) first in QGIS or an online converter
Lat/long swapped: points land in the wrong hemisphere — re-map the columns
Sign dropped: western longitudes (and southern latitudes) must be negative — -123.1, not 123.1
Degrees-minutes-seconds: convert to decimal degrees (DD = D + M/60 + S/3600)
Commas inside a field: wrap text values containing commas in double quotes so columns don't shift
Blank rows above the header: delete them so the first row is the header

sections.h2: Example: mapping a soil grid
sections.body: You have a 400-sample soil grid as a CSV with Sample ID, Lat, Long, and Au (ppb). Import it, map the coordinates, assign the Anomalies role, size the markers by the Au column so anomalies read instantly, label your top samples, add the claims boundary for context, and export Landscape 16:9. A spreadsheet becomes a presentation-ready anomaly map in a few minutes.

sections.h2: What the app does with your data

box.title: Can and cannot verify
box.html:
Can: plot points from your coordinates and keep every column as a property for labels.
Cannot: reproject from a local grid or UTM for you (convert to decimal degrees first), or validate the underlying assays/QA-QC. The map reflects exactly the data you import.

sections.h2: Related guides
sections.html: Use this with the drill results map guide and the mining claims map guide, or import vector files with the GeoJSON / Shapefile guide. Finish with the export to PDF guide.

faqs.q: What column names are recognized automatically?
faqs.a: For latitude: Lat, Latitude, lat, latitude, Y, POINT_Y. For longitude: Long, Longitude, lon, long, longitude, X, POINT_X. Anything else you assign manually in the Column Mapper dropdowns.

faqs.q: My data is in UTM, not latitude/longitude. Can I import it?
faqs.a: Not directly — Exploration Maps expects decimal degrees (WGS84). Convert first: open the CSV in QGIS, set the layer CRS to your UTM zone (e.g. EPSG:32610 for UTM Zone 10N), and export as GeoJSON in EPSG:4326. For small datasets, an online UTM-to-decimal converter works.

faqs.q: Can one CSV hold both drill collars and soil samples?
faqs.a: Import one layer per CSV. If a single file mixes types, split it into separate CSVs and import each as its own layer so you can style and role them independently.

faqs.q: What happens to my extra columns (Grade, Depth, Hole ID)?
faqs.a: They're preserved as feature properties on each point. They aren't shown on the map by default, but appear when you click a point — copy any value into the label or subtext to display it.

faqs.q: Can I import a CSV from Leapfrog, GEMS, or a mine-planning system?
faqs.a: Yes, as long as the export includes decimal-degree coordinate columns. If your system uses a local grid or UTM, reproject to WGS84 decimal degrees (e.g. via QGIS) before importing.

faqs.q: Does importing a CSV change the original file?
faqs.a: No. The import reads your CSV into the map; your source spreadsheet is untouched. The map stores its own copy of the points and properties.

title: How to Create an NI 43-101 Compliant Map
metaDescription: Step-by-step guide to creating map figures that meet NI 43-101 technical report standards. Covers required map elements, export quality, title block fields, and QP review requirements.
directAnswer: To create an NI 43-101 compliant map, enable the title block with project name, date, and projection reference, add a scale bar and north arrow, configure the legend, apply the Technical theme, and export as a high-resolution PDF. Exploration Maps includes all required map elements and applies industry-standard NI 43-101 styling by default.

sections.h2: What NI 43-101 Requires for Map Figures
sections.body: National Instrument 43-101 (Standards of Disclosure for Mineral Projects) does not prescribe a rigid cartographic standard, but QPs (Qualified Persons) and securities regulators expect map figures in technical reports to contain:
items: Title block: project name, property name, company name, map date, and figure number
Scale bar with distance in kilometres or metres appropriate to the map scale
Legend explaining all symbols and colours used
Datum and projection reference (e.g., NAD83 / UTM Zone 10N or WGS84)
Map type label (e.g., Property Location Map, Mineral Claims Map, Drill Hole Location Map)

sections.h2: Step 1: Choose the Right Export Ratio and Page Size
sections.body: NI 43-101 technical reports are submitted as PDFs, typically formatted to Letter or A4 paper size. Maps are embedded as figures — either full-page (Letter Landscape) or half-page figures within text. In Exploration Maps, select the Letter Portrait or Letter Landscape export ratio in the Export section. Letter Landscape is standard for full-page map figures. For maps intended to sit alongside descriptive text, Letter Portrait with the map occupying the upper portion of the page is common.

sections.h2: Step 2: Enable the Title Block with Required Fields
sections.body: Click the title text on the map to edit it inline. Enter the project name as the main title and the map type (e.g., 'Property Location Map') as the subtitle. In Design → Text & Metadata, fill in: Map Date (the date the data was compiled, not the export date), Company Name, and Figure Number if applicable. Upload the company logo using the logo uploader in the Layers section. The QP often requires the coordinate system in the title block — add this in the Map Notes field.

sections.h2: Step 3: Add Scale Bar and North Arrow
sections.body: Toggle on both the Scale Bar and North Arrow in the Design section. These are mandatory elements in every NI 43-101 map figure. The scale bar calculates the displayed distance automatically from the current zoom level and updates if you pan or zoom — always set your final map frame before exporting so the scale bar reflects the correct distance. The north arrow is placed automatically in the corner of the map frame.

sections.h2: Step 4: Configure the Legend
sections.body: The legend is generated automatically from your layer names and styles. Rename each layer in the layer card to a descriptive name that will appear in the legend — 'Mineral Claims' rather than 'layer_1', 'Drill Collars — 2024 Program' rather than 'drills_q3.csv'. For NI 43-101 maps, every symbol and colour used on the map must be identified in the legend. If you have multiple claim blocks with different colours, each should be a separate named layer.

sections.h2: Step 5: Apply the Technical Theme
sections.body: In the Design section, select the Technical — Sharp Borders theme. This theme is calibrated to the visual conventions used in Canadian technical reports: clean line weights, a light background, high-contrast claim boundaries, and a neutral colour palette that reproduces clearly when printed. The Investor — Navy & White theme is acceptable for investor-facing technical reports but is less conventional in formal regulatory submissions.

sections.h2: Step 6: Export at Submission Quality
sections.body: Export as PDF for formal NI 43-101 technical report submission. PDFs from Exploration Maps are vector-quality — lines, labels, and boundaries remain crisp at any zoom level when viewed in a PDF reader or printed. For PNG exports, use the 2× or 3× setting for sufficient resolution at the figure's intended print size. A Letter Landscape PNG at 3× is approximately 3300 × 2550 px — suitable for printing at 300 DPI on Letter paper.

faqs.q: What map elements are required in an NI 43-101 technical report?
faqs.a: The QP is responsible for ensuring figures meet the technical standards required under NI 43-101 and Form 43-101F1. Standard required elements are: title block (project name, company, date, figure number), north arrow, scale bar, legend, and a datum/projection reference. The exact figure list depends on the report type — a resource estimate report will require different maps than a preliminary economic assessment.

faqs.q: What resolution does a QP require for map figures?
faqs.a: There is no mandated resolution in NI 43-101, but industry practice is that map figures should be legible at the print size at which they will appear in the report. For PNG exports, 300 DPI at the intended print size is the accepted standard. Exploration Maps PDF exports are vector-quality and resolution-independent. PNG exports should use the 2× or 3× setting.

faqs.q: Does Exploration Maps support the coordinate system references required in NI 43-101?
faqs.a: All data in Exploration Maps is handled in WGS84 (EPSG:4326). If your QP requires a specific projection reference in the title block (e.g., NAD83 / UTM Zone 10N), add this as a manual note in the Map Notes field in Design → Text & Metadata. The underlying geographic accuracy is WGS84 — the projection statement is a labelling requirement for standard investor-facing map figures.

faqs.q: What colour conventions are expected for mineral claims in NI 43-101 maps?
faqs.a: The conventional colour for mineral claims in Canadian NI 43-101 reports is blue — a medium blue stroke with a light blue semi-transparent fill at 20–25% opacity. This is applied automatically when you assign the Claims role in Exploration Maps, and it matches the convention recognized by QPs, securities lawyers, and regulators across the industry.

faqs.q: Can I include multiple map figures from the same project in one Exploration Maps session?
faqs.a: Yes. Create multiple maps for the same project by adjusting the zoom level, visible layers, and export ratio for each figure. Common NI 43-101 figure sets include: a regional location map (zoomed out to show the province or state), a property location map (showing the claims boundary and surrounding area), and a detailed drill hole location map. Export each figure separately as a numbered PDF for insertion into the report document.
