title: What to Include on a Mining Claim Map
metaDescription: A practical checklist of what belongs on a mining claim map — claim boundaries, labels, scale, legend, context, and disclaimers — for investor and report use.
directAnswer: A mining claim map should include claim boundaries with clear styling, claim labels, a project title block, a legend, a scale bar and north arrow, geographic context (roads and water), and a note directing readers to the official registry for tenure verification.

sections.h2: Start with the claim boundaries
sections.body: The claim boundaries are the subject of the map. Style them with the conventional blue outline and light fill so they read as mineral tenure, and use separate layers and colours if you need to distinguish staked, optioned, or applied-for ground. Keep the boundaries the most visually prominent element on the page.

sections.h2: Label the claims
sections.body: Add labels so a reader can connect each block to a tenure number or a name used in the text. Unlabelled polygons force the reader to guess, which undermines a map meant for investors or reviewers.

sections.h2: Add a title block and date
sections.body: Include the project name, the company name, and a map date. Tenure changes over time, so a date tells the reader how current the land position is. A project number is useful for reports that reference multiple maps.

sections.h2: Include a legend, scale bar, and north arrow
sections.body: These three elements make a map readable and credible. The legend explains the colours, the scale bar gives a sense of size, and the north arrow orients the reader. Maps without them look like working screenshots rather than finished figures.

sections.h2: Provide geographic context
sections.body: Roads, water bodies, and nearby place names help a reader understand access and location. Add them as low-opacity context layers so they support the claims without competing with them.

sections.h2: Add a registry verification note
sections.html: Because a claim map is a visualization, not an ownership record, include a short note telling readers to confirm tenure details with the official government registry. For a full walkthrough of building the map, see how to make a mining claims map, or the BC mineral claims map tool for a province-specific workflow.

faqs.q: What is the standard colour for claims on a map?
faqs.a: A medium blue outline with a light, semi-transparent blue fill is the convention used in most technical reports and is recognized by investors and reviewers.

faqs.q: Does a claim map prove ownership?
faqs.a: No. A claim map is a visualization. Current ownership, status, and tenure must be verified through the official government mineral titles registry.

faqs.q: What map elements do investors expect?
faqs.a: At minimum a clear claim outline, labels, a legend, a scale bar, a north arrow, and a title block with the project name and date.

title: How to Turn Public Mineral Claim Data Into a Project Map
metaDescription: Turn public mineral claim data into a clean project map — find the data, import it, style the claims, add context, and export for decks and reports.
directAnswer: To turn public mineral claim data into a project map, download or search the claim boundaries, import them as GeoJSON, Shapefile, or KML, tag the layer as claims for standard styling, add context and a title block, then export as PNG or PDF.

sections.h2: Find the public claim data
sections.body: Most jurisdictions publish mineral claim boundaries through a government mineral titles portal. You can usually download tenure as Shapefile or KML, and in some cases search by holder or company name. This public data is the raw material for your map.

sections.h2: Import it into a mapping tool
sections.html: Bring the data into Exploration Maps, which reads Shapefile, GeoJSON, KML, and CSV. If your download is a Shapefile, you can import it directly — see Shapefile to map for that workflow. If you do not have a file, you can search claims by company name where the data supports it.

sections.h2: Style the claims
sections.body: Tag the imported layer as claims to apply the conventional outline and fill automatically. Use separate layers and colours for different claim blocks so each is distinct in the legend.

sections.h2: Add context and project details
sections.body: Add roads and water for geographic context, a title block with the project name and date, claim labels, and your company logo. These turn raw boundaries into a complete, readable project map.

sections.h2: Frame and export
sections.body: Set the ratio for your output — 16:9 for slides, letter or A4 for reports — frame the view, and export a PNG or PDF with the legend, scale bar, and north arrow included.

sections.h2: Verify before you rely on it
sections.body: Public data can lag behind the official record. Confirm current ownership, status, and tenure through the applicable government mineral titles registry before relying on the map for staking, transactions, or filings.

faqs.q: Where does public mineral claim data come from?
faqs.a: It is published by government mineral titles registries, which typically offer tenure boundaries as Shapefile or KML downloads and sometimes a search by company or holder name.

faqs.q: What format should I import?
faqs.a: GeoJSON imports most reliably, but Shapefile, KML, and CSV are all supported. Convert non-WGS84 data to WGS84 (EPSG:4326) first.

faqs.q: Is the resulting map official?
faqs.a: No. It is a visualization of public data. Verify current tenure details through the official registry before relying on them.

title: How to Map Drill Results for a Junior Mining News Release
metaDescription: Build a clear drill results figure for a junior mining news release: plot collars, highlight the headline intercepts, show targets and claims for context, and export a clean PNG or PDF that reads on a phone.
directAnswer: For a news-release drill map, plot collars from a CSV or GeoJSON, label the holes, badge the headline intercepts (grade × width), add the target and claim boundaries for context, keep the legend and title block clean, and export a PNG for the release and website or a news-release figure PDF. The map should be legible at the small size it appears in an email or on a phone.

ctaTop.text: Import your file and export an investor-ready map.
ctaTop.sub: Drop in your collars and build a release-ready figure in minutes.

ctaBottom.text: Turn drill data into a clean news-release figure.
ctaBottom.sub: Open the editor, label your best holes, and export a clean figure.

sections.h2: Why the map carries the news release
sections.body: In a junior mining news release, the drill map is often the first thing readers look at — before the body text. A clear map showing where the holes are and which intercepts matter helps the result land with investors who skim. A confusing map buries the news.

sections.h2: What you need
checklist: Drill collar locations (hole ID + decimal-degree coordinates) as CSV or GeoJSON
The headline intercepts you'll highlight (grade × width)
The target outline and your claim boundaries for context
Your logo, the date, and the project name

sections.h2: Step 1: Plot the drill collars
sections.body: Import collars from a CSV or GeoJSON — typically hole ID, latitude, longitude — and assign the Drillholes role. Keep the symbols consistent so the drilling pattern is easy to follow. See the CSV import guide if your coordinates need attention.

image.alt: Drill collars plotted as consistent markers over a target area
image.caption: Collars plotted with the Drillholes role

sections.h2: Step 2: Highlight the headline intercepts
sections.body: Add Badge Label callouts next to the holes that drive the story — grade over width, e.g. '32m @ 6.1 g/t Au'. Resist labelling every hole; spotlight the results investors should remember so attention goes where it should.

image.alt: Two holes badge-labelled with their best intercepts standing out from the pattern
image.caption: Badging only the headline holes keeps the story clear

cta.text: Turn drill data into a clean news-release figure.
cta.sub: Badge your best holes so the numbers stand out at a glance.

sections.h2: Step 3: Show the target and claim context
sections.body: Add the target outline and your claim boundaries so readers can see the holes are testing the intended target and sit on the company's ground. Context is what turns a set of dots into an interpretable result.

sections.h2: Step 4: Keep it clean for a wide audience
sections.body: News releases reach non-technical readers. Use a clear basemap, a concise legend, a readable title block with your logo, and a scale bar and north arrow. Check it's legible at the size it will appear in an email or on a phone.

sections.h2: Step 5: Export the figure
sections.body: Export a PNG sized for email and web, or a news-release figure PDF if the map is also a report attachment. Reuse the same figure in your investor deck so the story stays visually consistent across the release, the website, and the presentation.

sections.h2: Example: a discovery-hole release
sections.body: Your release leads with one discovery hole and two strong step-outs. Plot all collars, badge the three headline holes with grade × width, outline the target and your claim, switch to Satellite to show the setting, add a clean title block with the date, and export a PNG plus a figure PDF — ready for the wire, the website, and the deck.

sections.h2: Disclosure and verification

box.title: Follow your disclosure obligations
box.html: The map reflects the data you enter — it does not validate assays, true widths, or QA/QC. Ensure intercepts and any claim/target context follow your QP's guidance and applicable securities disclosure rules, and verify claim boundaries with the official registry. Exploration Maps is a visualization tool, not the official registry.

sections.h2: Related guides
sections.html: Build the underlying map with the drill results map guide, fix coordinate issues with the CSV import guide, reuse the figure with the investor presentation guide, and export with the PDF export guide. The full feature set is on the drill results map tool page.

faqs.q: What data do I need for a news-release drill map?
faqs.a: At minimum the collar locations (hole ID and decimal-degree coordinates), plus the key intercepts you'll highlight as callouts, and ideally the target outline and claim boundaries for context.

faqs.q: What format should the figure be?
faqs.a: A PNG sized for email and web works for most releases; a news-release figure PDF is useful when the map is also a report attachment. Include a legend, scale bar, north arrow, and title block.

faqs.q: How many holes should I label?
faqs.a: Only the ones that carry the story. Badge your headline intercepts and keep the rest as plain markers, so the result a reader should remember is unmistakable at a small size.

faqs.q: How do I keep the map readable for investors?
faqs.a: Use a clean basemap, a concise legend, and a readable title block, highlight only the intercepts that matter, and verify it's legible at the small size it appears in a release or on a phone.

faqs.q: Can I reuse the figure in my investor deck?
faqs.a: Yes — that's recommended. Export the same map for the release and at 16:9 for the deck so your drilling story looks consistent everywhere. See the investor presentation guide.

title: Shapefile, KML, GeoJSON, and CSV: Which File Types Matter for Exploration Mapping?
metaDescription: Understand Shapefile, KML, GeoJSON, and CSV for exploration mapping — what each is good for, when to use it, and how to get a clean map from each.
directAnswer: Shapefile and GeoJSON are best for claim and target boundaries, KML is common from Google Earth and government exports, and CSV suits drill collars and sample points with coordinate columns. Exploration Maps imports all four.

sections.h2: Why file types matter
sections.body: Exploration data arrives in several formats depending on where it came from — a government tenure download, a colleague’s Google Earth file, a lab’s spreadsheet. Knowing what each format is good for saves time and avoids conversion headaches when you build a map.

sections.h2: Shapefile
sections.body: The Shapefile is the long-standing GIS standard for boundaries like claims and targets. It is actually several files together (.shp, .shx, .dbf and others), so keep them as a set. It is the most common format from government mineral titles portals.

sections.h2: GeoJSON
sections.body: GeoJSON is a single, modern, text-based format that is easy to share and imports cleanly. It works well for claim boundaries, target outlines, roads, and any polygon or line data. If you can choose, GeoJSON is often the smoothest option.

sections.h2: KML
sections.body: KML comes from Google Earth and many government exports. It is handy for quick sketches and shared boundaries, though styling embedded in KML may be simplified on import. It is well suited to outlines and points you already have in Google Earth.

sections.h2: CSV
sections.body: CSV is a spreadsheet export, ideal for point data like drill collars and sample locations that have latitude and longitude columns. A column mapper assigns the coordinate fields on import. CSV is the natural format for results that start life in Excel.

sections.h2: Getting a clean map from any of them
sections.html: Whatever the format, the workflow is the same: import, tag the layer by role for styling, add context and a title block, and export. See Shapefile to map for files you already have, or mining exploration map software for the full picture. A note on projection: convert data to WGS84 (EPSG:4326) before importing for the most reliable result.

faqs.q: Which file format is best for exploration mapping?
faqs.a: GeoJSON is often the smoothest for boundaries and lines; Shapefile is the GIS standard and common from government portals; CSV is best for drill collars and samples; KML is common from Google Earth. Exploration Maps supports all four.

faqs.q: Do I need to convert my files?
faqs.a: Usually not — Shapefile, GeoJSON, KML, and CSV import directly. Convert to WGS84 (EPSG:4326) first if your data is in another coordinate system.

faqs.q: How do I map data from a spreadsheet?
faqs.a: Export it as CSV with latitude and longitude columns and use the column mapper on import to assign the coordinate fields.

title: How to Create a Mineral Exploration Map for an Investor Deck
metaDescription: Plan the maps in a mining investor deck — which map belongs on which slide, how to keep them consistent, and how to export them at 16:9 for presentations.
directAnswer: A mining investor deck usually needs a location map, a claims map, and a results or target map. Build them from one project with a consistent theme, size them to 16:9, and export PNG at 2× so they stay sharp on screen and projector.

sections.h2: Think about the deck, not just one map
sections.html: Most decks need a small set of maps that tell a sequence: where the project is, what the company controls, and what the exploration has found. Rather than make each one in isolation, plan them together so they share a style. For the step-by-step of a single presentation map, see how to create an exploration map for an investor presentation.

sections.h2: The location slide
sections.body: Open with a location map that places the project in its region — near a recognized district, relative to towns and infrastructure. This answers the first question an investor asks and sets up everything that follows.

sections.h2: The claims slide
sections.html: Follow with a claims map showing the land position. Keep it clean and labelled so the extent of the ground is obvious. See the claims map workflow for how to build it.

sections.h2: The results or target slide
sections.html: Show the exploration story with a target map or a drill results map, highlighting the intercepts or anomalies that matter. This is usually the slide investors study most.

sections.h2: Keep the maps consistent
sections.body: Use the same theme, basemap, and title style across every map in the deck. Consistent maps look deliberate and professional; a mix of styles looks assembled from whatever was on hand. Building them from one saved project makes this easy.

sections.h2: Export for presentation
sections.html: Size maps to a 16:9 landscape ratio and export PNG at 2× resolution so they stay sharp on a projector or large screen. For the full toolset, see mining map software.

faqs.q: Which maps belong in a mining investor deck?
faqs.a: Typically a location map, a claims map showing the land position, and a results or target map highlighting the exploration story. The exact set depends on the project stage.

faqs.q: What size should deck maps be?
faqs.a: A 16:9 landscape ratio matches standard slides. Export PNG at 2× resolution so the maps stay sharp on screen and projector.

faqs.q: How do I keep the maps consistent?
faqs.a: Build them from one saved project and apply the same theme, basemap, and title style across each map so the deck looks cohesive.
